title: Load Growth Guide
summary: Understand how current load, annual growth, time horizon, and planning headroom affect future power requirements.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool projects how a power load may grow over time and adds planning headroom so the design is not sized only for day-one conditions.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch power/runtime pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning UPS capacity, battery runtime, branch loading, rack power, or future expansion where load is expected to increase.
bullets: Early power and runtime planning.
Expansion or refresh review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining assumptions to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each visible input represents in practical power and runtime planning terms.

items.label: Current Load (kW)
items.description: The current measured or estimated load before future growth is applied.
items.whyItMatters: This is the baseline that all future projections build from.
items.commonMistake: Starting with a rough guess instead of a measured or normalized load.

links.label: Normalize watts / VA / amps first
links.note: Convert mixed equipment ratings before projecting growth.

items.label: Annual Growth (%)
items.description: The expected percentage increase in load each year.
items.whyItMatters: Growth rate determines how quickly the system can outgrow the original design.
items.commonMistake: Using zero growth because the first phase looks acceptable.

items.label: Time Horizon (Years)
items.description: The number of years the design should support before another major review.
items.whyItMatters: Longer horizons require more future capacity.
items.commonMistake: Sizing only for installation day and ignoring lifecycle needs.

items.label: Planning Headroom (%)
items.description: Extra capacity reserved above the projected load.
items.whyItMatters: Headroom protects against estimation error, load spikes, and unplanned equipment.
items.commonMistake: Treating projected load as the exact capacity target.

links.label: Carry projected load into UPS runtime
links.note: UPS runtime should use the load you expect to support, not only today’s load.

sections.title: How this connects to the design flow
body: Power and runtime tools are strongest when the results are carried forward instead of treated as one-off calculations. Use related Knowledge Base references when one value depends on another planning assumption.
bullets: Start with clear load math before runtime sizing.
Carry projected or worst-case loads into UPS and battery planning.
Review degraded or redundant scenarios before treating runtime as final.

links.label: Use projected load in UPS runtime

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy results indicate projected load and headroom are still manageable. Watch or Risk means the future load may exceed the power, UPS, or runtime envelope unless capacity is added or assumptions are revised.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions appear manageable for planning purposes.
Watch: the design may still work, but margin, growth, or failure behavior deserves review.
Risk: add capacity, reduce load, increase runtime reserve, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most power/runtime planning problems come from sizing one value in isolation while ignoring load growth, conversion losses, battery derating, redundancy behavior, and real operating margin.
bullets: Sizing only for day-one load instead of projected or peak load.
Treating advertised capacity as fully usable runtime capacity.
Ignoring inverter efficiency, battery health, temperature, and reserve margin.
Forgetting that redundant capacity may not be normal usable capacity.
Failing to document assumptions before the system is built.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer runtime charts, electrical design review, code compliance review, battery documentation, or site-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor runtime curves and battery specifications still matter.
Actual power factor, load behavior, and environmental conditions can change results.
Battery age, temperature, maintenance, and discharge limits affect usable energy.
A design with documented headroom is easier to support, expand, and troubleshoot.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, electrical/code compliance review, manufacturer validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual UPS, inverter, battery, load, and electrical system being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for runtime, failover, maintenance, and growth.
Document assumptions before implementation.
